text: 0.1g of fat. saturated fat, cholesterol, and omega3 are doing most of the work. for dairy, the fat side can either add richness or drag the whole thing down.
5g of carbs. sugar and glycemic index matter most here. for a dairy food, this is doing the right stuff.
3.4g of protein. bioavailability and amino acid quality are both strong. useful protein can rescue a lot of weaker traits here.
vitamin b12 at 20% daily value stands out most. that is only mild support overall.
calcium at 10% daily value stands out most. useful, but still pretty limited.

pros first: easy protein delivery for normal diets because dairy gets real credit for convenience. easy to fit into familiar meals because that lowers friction for regular use. slots into meals very easily because practical versatility matters a lot for dairy.

on the weaker side: processing changes the category a lot because the label alone does not tell the whole story. fat quality varies wildly across dairy because some dairy is clean, some really is not. some versions get dense fast on fat, sodium, or both because the category swings a lot by format.
S tier. Best when the protein payoff or fermentation angle outweighs the fat and sodium tradeoffs.
